speaker: interviewee
text: [TONE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is October 23rd, 1996. Survivor, Charles Mudryk. Interviewer, Marilyn Feingold. City of Southfield, state of Michigan, country, USA. Language, English. Name at birth, Aaron Mudryk. My name is Marilyn Rita Feingold-- M-A-R-I-L-Y-N R-I-T-A F-E-I-N-G-O-L-D. And I have the privilege today of interviewing Charles Mudryk. Today's date is October 23rd, 1996. We're in the city of Southfield, Michigan, The United States of America. This interview will be conducted in English. Can you tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Charles Mudryk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-U-D-R-Y-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What was your name at birth? And could you spell your first and last name for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My name at birth was Aaron. A-A-R-O-N. And last name, M-U-D-R-Y-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents and my brother and sister used to call me Arle.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And who were you named after? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am not sure, but I think it's after my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born? Your birthday?

speaker: interviewer
text: And that would make you how old now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 60 years old. Just turned 60 in April.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. What city were you born in, and could you spell that for me as best you can?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dombrowitz. D-O-M-B-R-O-W-I-T-Z, I believe, phonetically.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the country of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ukraine. It used to be-- at one time belong to Poland. Then it was Ukraine, so I would imagine it was Ukraine when I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We're going to start with pre-war, but pre-war was very short for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about where you lived? Do you remember how the town was, or any little thing like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember certain things, like I remember my parents owned a big house, and we had more like a farm area. Big acreage. What I remember, I must have been about three years old. I was sitting in a tree-- [CLEARS THROAT] excuse me-- and I had a coin in my hand. And I put it in my mouth, and it went in, and it-- I didn't swallow it, and it was stuck in the middle. And I remember my father carrying me to the doctor. And-- and I finally threw up, and I threw up the coin. That was one of the-- I also remember when the Germans came into our town on motorcycles, and I was-- I was fascinated by the motorcycles. And I would go up there and touch it, and they were very friendly when they first came in. And I remember that part of our-- our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your parents do for a living? You lived out in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: My father used to deal in cattle. He used to buy and sell cattle and-- and horses and that type of thing. He and my uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So livestock?

speaker: interviewee
text: Livestock, right. [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's name and your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Avrum, and my mother's name was Chava.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you told me something interesting. What does Mudryk mean? It has a special meaning.

speaker: interviewee
text: When my sister came over here from Israel-- about three or four years ago, I think it was-- she-- she was telling me that Mudryk in Ukrainian means the wise man. So I don't know exactly how that came about. But from what they were saying, that my grandfather was the wise man, and I guess that's how we got the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children were in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my immediate family?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your siblings.

speaker: interviewee
text: A brother. I have a brother and a sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest sister, the oldest one, is-- she's just 68 now. And my brother must be 64 now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their-- that's their ages. And their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: And their names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sarah and David.

speaker: interviewer
text: David. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Let's talk just a little bit more about what you remember. Tiny bits that you remember before the war. What language did you speak. at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke Yiddish. But right now, my Yiddish isn't too good. Yiddish, and I must have spoken Ukrainian. But I don't speak Ukrainian. I don't remember too much about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You said you lived in a big house. Did other people, besides your family, live in the house, too? Did you have aunts or uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not in the immediate house. I guess there was, like, almost, like, a duplex. And on one side, my aunt and uncle lived, and we lived on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there cousins as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. A female cousin and a male cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Your family, were they a religious family? Were you Orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Orthodox. I don't know how religious. I remember going to the synagogue on Saturdays and holidays. I remember that. But as far as the extent of their religion, I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember celebrating Shabbos in the house at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember my mother bentching on Friday night.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a nice dinner, that type of thing? Or do recall any?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOB] [DRINKS] [COUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [CLEARS THROAT] So you do remember a little bit about Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot about Shabbos. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What else do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CLEARS THROAT] Well, I remember [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the ghetto that the Germans sent us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you. Because you started talking about the motorcycles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Germans coming in, and they were very friendly at first.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, did the grownups start to act differently? Do you remember people talking differently all of a sudden?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CLEARS THROAT] They were-- I think they were anxious and nervous. I didn't exactly know why, but they were concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- and you remember going up to the motorcycles and touching? And they let you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And touching them. Yeah. They were very friendly when they first came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] You said you remember being sent to the ghetto. What can you tell me about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CLEARS THROAT] They-- they told us we had to move, and they were going to relocate us. And they moved us, I think, to a different part of town. And then from there, they were going to-- they were lining us up to take us to-- on the trains, to relocate us. They wouldn't tell us where. And as we were marching, my father knew that something was wrong, and he grabbed me and my brother and sister and mother, and we started running. And they started shooting after us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And no one was-- no one was hit?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We weren't hit, but we could hear the bullets and the firearms going off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you were marched to the trains, how long were you in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about six months or less. Somewhere around there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were moved to somebody's house. Did you know whose house it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I have no idea whose house that was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the people who were already there, did you live in the house with them? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it crowded, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was kind of crowded, I think. But I don't remember too much of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the food? Did you-- did they-- were you able to get food at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, at that point, there was no problem getting food and--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were still able to.

speaker: interviewee
text: --things like that. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And this was still in Dombrowitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dombrowitz, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You ran. Where did your dad run you to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until we-- the woods. It was a field, and then the woods began. So that's where we wound up in.

speaker: interviewer
text: You ran into the woods. Did anybody chase after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Once we got into the woods, I don't think they-- they finally gave up. You know, they weren't going to-- because they were marching up. There were a lot of people in the lines, so I mean, I don't think they would leave the lines and let the other people escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody-- any other families run with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my uncle and cousin-- a male cousin-- ran with us. But my aunt and her daughter didn't-- didn't want to go. So they remained with the-- with the rest of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they went on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: They went on the train. And from there, I imagine, the concentration camps. But we didn't hear from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember-- you hid in the woods? Do you remember the first night in the woods at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Or know what it was like after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the first night, but I do remember the cold nights where, you know, we tried to keep warm. And it was difficult. Used coats or whatever we had to keep warm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the ground, [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the open.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the open.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever remember being in a bunker or in a barn, or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When we-- we didn't stay in one place. We had to move from one area to the other so we wouldn't be detected by the Germans or the Ukrainians. So sometimes we'd sleep in a barn and-- whenever there was shelter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to eat? What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: We ate-- [LAUGHS] in the summer, I remember-- were the berries. You had strawberries and blueberries and so on. And also, my parents or one of the adults would go and-- to a farmer. And if the farmer was friendly enough, they would give us food. And that's how my parents got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went out looking for food. And from what we understood later on, they were captured by-- by Ukrainians, not the Germans-- and dragged through the street on a horse. They tied them up until they-- they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and how-- how did you find out that this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- I guess later on, some of the adults told us. They found out what had happened. See, a lot of the Ukrainians would capture Jews and turn them over to the Germans for a reward or something. They would do it just for the fun of it. It was a-- a sport to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were with your brother and sister and yourself, and that was it? Three little kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Well, there were other adults in there. There was a group maybe of 20 people who lived in the same area with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there like a leader of the group? Do you remember anybody like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] There may have been several leaders. I don't think there was only one that would confer when we should move, and what we should do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they start to fight or anything with the Ukrainians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did. Well, they would sabotage a lot of time. My brother and some of the other men would sabotage bridges and so on. So they did have some weapons with them. And this was later on during the war. It didn't happen initially, but later on it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you with your parents before they were killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too long. Maybe about a year, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're talking maybe what, 1940--

speaker: interviewee
text: '42 Or '43. Somewhere around there.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Who took care of you?

speaker: interviewer
text: When, during the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: During the-- when you were in the woods, after your parents were gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my brother and sister and the other adults--

speaker: interviewer
text: Other adults.

speaker: interviewee
text: --would take care of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when anybody-- you know, you came across strangers periodically, I'm sure. Or if you knew of Ukrainians in the area, what were you told to do? How were you told to behave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to be quiet and don't say anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you told to hide anywhere, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah. We were-- if we heard somebody coming, we would hide. But sometimes they surprised you and came from a different direction. And then, you know, we were at their mercy.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your sense of everything going on, were you, like, always scared, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you were very young. You were only six? Five or six years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other children besides your brother and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Come to think of it, there weren't-- there's-- there was my cousin before he and his father were killed also. I don't think there were any other children. Most of them were adults there.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how old was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was around he was around 14 at the time when he got killed, I think. He was a little older.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember their names? Your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my uncle's name, Asher, but I don't remember the cousin's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he your mother's brother or your father's?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So his last name would've been--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Bechek.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Bechek. Also. [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAPERS RUSTLING]

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you-- do you remember anything else about the woods? Do you remember how you-- could you wash up? Could you sleep any place warm, ever? Things like that? I remember you, in the pre-interview, had mentioned that you had a problem with lice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember when the war was over, and-- and they-- they shaved our heads and disinfected us, so to speak. But I remember having lice and having itching all over the place. And-- where did we sleep? Most of the time, we slept outdoors. But when we moved from place to place, we would sleep in barns. or any-- usually in barns, If we weren't detected. We had to be careful not to be detected. But usually when we moved was at night. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened if anybody got sick? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember anybody really getting sick, like having a cold or the flu or anything, which is unusual.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you would think being in the woods--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that something would happen. But you don't recall that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall that-- anything like that happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a point, I believe, you were separated from your brother and sister. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. After the war-- or the war had ended, and I went with this one couple, and my brother and sister went with another couple, and we were separated. And what had happened was, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this couple that I was with were communists. And they were afraid, after the war, that some-- they-- they would capture them, for some reason, and do something to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- they left me at a train station, in the city of Rowno, which is the Ukraine. And they said, we'll be right back. Well, 10, 15, 20 minutes later, nothing happened, and I was left alone. So I decided-- it was at night-- I slept in the train station by myself. And the next morning, I got up, and I was wandering around the city. The little town. And I didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I sat down near a-- a house, and this couple took me in. Their names were the Achtenbaums. They were Jewish, and they took me in. And I lived with them until I came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were liberated. You started to tell me, you had to be shaved because of the lice. Who were you liberated by?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Russians. The Russians came in, and-- and before we could do anything, they-- they made sure that we were clean and so on. So I remember having to go through--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then this other couple, were they also a Jewish couple? The couple that abandoned you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. They were the ones with us-- they were in the woods with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So they were [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Evidently, they were running. They were running for some reason, or being afraid of being captured as being Communist. I don't under-- quite understand why. But-- And they just-- just left me alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then this other couple came.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, and took me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take you to? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they-- they had their-- they lived in an apartment, I guess it was. And I lived with them. And from there, from Rowno, we went to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: They decided they didn't want to be in Rowno?

speaker: interviewee
text: They decided we want-- right. They decided they weren't going to stay there, and they-- and we moved to France. And I lived in France from '45 to '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you just-- Do you remember the train ride or anything, going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I remember the train ride. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was-- for a kid, I've never been on a train before. I think it was a very exciting experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: They became like your parents to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They became like-- yeah. They were very close. And like I said, I stayed with them until '48, when my aunt brought me over here to Detroit.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed in Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You learned French.

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned French. Went to school-- elementary school-- there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did being Jewish ever become an issue in France?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, not really. In fact, we lived in the Jewish ghetto. Not a ghetto, but it's mostly Jewish people. And funny you should ask. My son just went there last year. He went to France and Paris and visited at the neighborhood, and it's still predominantly Jewish. It's been Jewish since the 1800s. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came to the United States. Your aunt found you. Did she actively search for you, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She put an ad in the Jewish In the And the Achtenbaums saw the ad, and that's how she got in touch with me. And they-- they wanted me to come to the United States with my aunt. I really didn't want to. I wanted to go and stay with them, because I was more attached to them. And that's how I came over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the trip over here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I sure do. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about it. Tell me about that trip.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on a-- on a boat called the Ernie Pyle. And this was in February, and it was a really rough winter. And I remember going on-- staying-- staying on deck and going up. And you could see the waves as high as the sky. And I was-- I was introduced to the candy bar, the Milky Way and the Snickers. And I was eating candy bars left and right. And I got so sick, I couldn't-- couldn't look at another candy bar. And I remember they gave us a medication for sea sickness that tasted like root beer. And ever since then, I cannot stand the smell of root beer because of the medication [INAUDIBLE]. But that trip on the boat lasted 14 days because of the rough weather. So I do remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you travel alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: I traveled alone, right. The Achtenbaums brought me on a-- took me to the train in Paris. And from Paris, we went to Le Havre, which is the port in France. And from there, we took the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- when you got here, did you keep in touch with them? With the Achtenbaums?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- we-- I did for a while, and then the Achtenbaums went to Israel. And after that, I-- I lost touch with them. And in fact, recently, about three years ago, Mrs. Achtenbaum wrote to me and corresponded. I corresponded a few times, but it's kind of hard to keep in touch when you haven't seen them for so long.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got sick on candy and on the boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the boat, right. [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you first saw the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw the Statue-- the Statue of Liberty. That was the sign that we were in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thought it was great. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And who came to pick you up at the port?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousin. My cousin Ida came to pick me up in New York. And she asked me what name did I want to use. And I said, my name is Carole. That was the name that they gave me in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, well, do you want another name? And I started-- I thought, and I said, well, Charles sounds pretty good, so why not pick Charles? That's how it became Charles. So you went from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Aaron to Carole to Charles.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aliases.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your cousin came and picked you up in New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you stay after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed at a hotel. I don't remember the name of the hotel. It was-- more like six hours. We didn't stay very long. And from there, we went on a train to Detroit. And she asked me what I wanted to drink on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, I'll have a beer. And she looked at me. A beer? I said OK, I'll have milk. She didn't realize that in France, children drink beer and wine for lunch and dinner without any-- anybody saying anything. That's part of the culture. [CHUCKLES]
